channel.title: Science
channel.description: The very best in news and research across the sciences.

item.title: A key Arctic science outpost finds itself tangled in a geopolitical web
item.description: Svalbard is warming faster than almost anywhere on Earth, but security concerns are tightening access to its glaciers, fjords, and sea floor

item.title: How an ancient continental breakup helped spawn the Antarctic Ice Sheet
item.description: Mantle waves and tectonic stretching lofted polar mountains into a deep freeze

item.title: Continental breakup–driven uplift instigated East Antarctic Ice Sheet formation | Science
item.description: Why Antarctica became glaciated ∼34 million years ago (Ma) remains debated, as relatively warm climates and sea temperatures appear inconsistent with ice sheet formation. Although a critical decline in CO2 is considered primarily responsible, evidence ...

item.title: Patterning human kidney organoids with synthetic Wnt-secreting organizers | Science
item.description: Human stem cell–derived miniature organs, including kidney organoids, reproduce aspects of tissue development but lack reliable spatial patterning. In embryos, spatial organization is often established by developmental organizers that generate ...

item.title: Membrane protein solubilization and structure determination using de novo–designed proteins | Science
item.description: Developing therapies and vaccines against integral membrane proteins is hindered by their extensive hydrophobic surfaces, which complicate production and structural analysis. Here, we describe a general deep learning–based design approach for ...

item.title: Institutionalizing AI diplomacy in Africa | Science
item.description: The contemporary landscape of artificial intelligence (AI) diplomacy in Africa is structurally bound to the continent’s history of science, technology, and innovation (STI) engagement. Since the nineteenth century, the African continent has been positioned primarily as a target of external scientific inquiry rather than an active participant in global knowledge production. Colonial-era scientific expeditions sought to catalog geological resources and ecological assets to feed the industrial economies of Western countries, establishing a systemic dependency that relegated Africa to the periphery of the global natural resource trade and other strategic mineral markets. This legacy shaped an international order in which technological advancements across North America, Europe, and East Asia frequently occur through the extraction of African resources, without corresponding technology transfer or local capacity building.

item.title: In Other Journals | Science
item.description: Editors’ selections from the current scientific literature

item.title: In Science Journals | Science
item.description: Highlights from the Science family of journals

item.title: The missing thriftiness | Science
item.description: Host-microbial coevolution and its influence on human health

item.title: Ecology of the gut microbiome | Science
item.description: Microbial competition can be harnessed to prevent and cure deadly diseases

item.title: The invisible hand | Science
item.description: Microbiota have a hidden role in mosquito-borne disease

item.title: Family stability sustains scientific talent | Science
item.description: Recent US immigration actions show how quickly legal pathways to permanent residence can become uncertain. The Department of State has suspended immigrant visa issuance for nationals of 75 listed countries while reviewing screening and vetting policies related to public-benefits reliance (1). US Citizenship and Immigration Services has also announced that adjustment of status will be granted only in extraordinary circumstances, signaling that many temporary residents seeking green cards may need to apply through consular processing abroad (2, 3). These policies do not only affect scientists, yet they raise a science-policy concern: Family instability can weaken the retention of foreign-born researchers.

item.title: Stop the biodiversity sampling arms race | Science
item.description: Global biodiversity is in crisis, with wild vertebrate populations declining by 69% since 1970 (1). At the same time, advances in high-throughput genetic sequencing and an increase in international collaborations have shifted biodiversity research toward data-intensive paradigms, in which single studies may now involve thousands of specimens and large quantities of species-level data (2). But must biodiversity breakthroughs depend on substantial biological sampling? In our opinion, the answer is no. Instead, we believe that study sample size should be determined on the basis of scientific justifications.

item.title: End “reusable” waste dumping in Africa | Science

item.title: WWII and the evolution of US federal research funding | Science
item.description: Many fundamental innovation policies were developed during World War II that persist in some form to this day

item.description: The nation’s research universities have traced an indelible path through the American story

item.title: Making time | Science
item.description: Flies seek out darkness to restart their biological clocks

item.title: The mechanics of liver regeneration | Science
item.description: A mechanosensitive ion channel regulates liver cell proliferation after injury

item.title: Computing in a memory with physics | Science
item.description: An artificial neural network built into a computer memory chip reconstructs the human cortex with high accuracy in real time

item.title: Local signals, systemic decline | Science
item.description: A high-fat diet affects tumor-to-nerve signaling and promotes cachexia in mice

item.title: On Zeppelin Mountain, sensors sniff Russian air | Science
item.description: The red cable car emblazoned with the logo of the Norwegian Polar Institute (NPI) swayed as it ascended the flank of Zeppelin Mountain above Svalbard’s Ny-Alesund settlement. The science base below receded into a scattering of brightly colored buildings on the shore of Kongsfjorden, the “king’s fjord.” Above loomed Norway’s Zeppelin Station, a squat building that has become one of the Arctic’s most important sentinels of atmospheric change.

item.title: 250 years of promise | Science
item.description: America has for its entire history been fascinated with science. Although the lofty ideals associated with establishing the country have mostly to do with personal liberty and equality, the founders were very aware of the importance of new knowledge, and ...

item.title: Lab-created ‘SpudCell’ marks ‘stunning’ step toward building life from scratch
item.description: A synthetic cell can now grow and divide—but it’s still far from alive

item.title: Rubin observatory begins a 10-year movie of the changing universe
item.description: World’s biggest digital camera will repeatedly scan the sky, spotting cosmic explosions while building up a deep map of billions of galaxies

item.title: NIH likely to award fewer grants as it races to spend 2026 budget
item.description: Government shutdown, staff shortages have complicated effort to meet 30 September deadline to spend funds

item.title: Why have papers by one of history’s most famous physicists been retracted?
item.description: Springer Nature has removed two studies by Max Planck. A bot may be to blame

item.title: How AI, $1 billion, and a transparent fish could transform neuroscience
item.description: The once-obscure Danionella fish takes center stage as a model research organism in a major new project funded by Howard Hughes Medical Institute

item.title: New NIH security rules for genomic data sets are slowing research, prompting workarounds
item.description: Data security experts say increased oversight is needed, but researchers are struggling to comply
